children.text_html: Example 1: Line/Area++ (Area, Dot, Line, Circle)
South Region Sales History

Æ
⬤ Æ Highlighter:
Æ
◯ Not Profitable
▲ Try changing the date granularity to quarterly or weekly.
Inspiration: Jennifer Dawes Tableau Tiny Tip Tuesday 9/17/24.

Æ Æ
Going beyond standard cool, with cool ++
Variable Highlighter ▶

So, what prompted all these chart ideas?Æ Æ
Credit goes to Jennifer Dawes and a Tableau Tiny Tip Tuesday in which she showed a highlighter done via dual axis with a colored circle on one axis. I thought, "I can do that circle without a dual-axis if I used a symbol in text." Then I thought, "Wait a second, that essentially gives me an extra mark." And then I thought, "Wait, if I do that on both axes, I can get two extra marks!" And then the ideas started flowing nonstop. So, this workbook is essentially showing a new way to think about existing functionality.Æ Æ
Æ
What's going on with this specific example?Æ Æ
Here we are showing a common dual-axis, the line-plus-area approach. Some people like the look of these, and you may think after implementing one that you are at your mark limit. Not true. You can add Faux Marks beyond the Real Marks for additional functionality. Here we have the dynamic highlighter from Jennifer's example as well as an indicator of whether a given month happened to be profitable. Note that the title, "Line/Area++" means "two steps beyond Line/Area".Æ
Æ
Also, that the dynamic highlighter could include any number of additional calculations like a Top 5, Percentiles, Quartiles, Quintiles, 1 sigma, 2 sigma, etc.Æ Æ
Æ
How did you do it?Æ Æ
The dots are Unicode symbols – a large black circle (⬤) resulting from a calculated field that ingests the highlight parameter. The rings are large white circles (◯) from a calculated field. Both fields return null if not applicable – that’s a key trick to these dynamic marks. This approach is leveraged in multiple examples.Æ
Æ
Why the South Region?Æ Æ
With Region filtered to South, the Max and Last highlight options produce different Faux Marks, so it was chosen to better illustrate the example data.Æ
Æ
Is there related material?Æ Æ
Here is Jennifer Dawes' Tiny Tableau Tip:Æ
https://www.herdata.net/post/tableau-tiny-tip-tuesday-hiding-marksÆ
Æ

What's going on with this specific example?Æ Æ Æ Æ
We have a few things happening here. First, we are showcasing that state images can be displayed in a trellis rather than with latitude and longitude. Thanks, Tore! We have Real Marks for those state shapes and their sales circles. The Faux Marks are the "Met Goal" dot and the 3x3 indicator grid.Æ Æ

What are you trying to show with the indicator grid?Æ Æ
The grid shows that our graphing limitation isn't Tableau; it's human perception. The vast majority of people cannot perceive a 3x3 grid of binary indicators in one glance. We're not AI bots, but in theory we could have one as an assistant that could quickly summarize the even crazier examples below:Æ

What about Fonts and Formatting?Æ Æ
As a general rule, the recommendation is to use Tableau Book for the best spacing. In this particular example, the Faux dots have a single space on the label prior to the dot to get them to render more on-center on server. Moving Beyond Dots and Circles
Æ

What's going on with this specific example?Æ Æ
The whole purpose of this workbook is to break you out of functionalÆ fixedness and get you to think of using Tableau in new ways. Functional fixedness is the tendency to  think of using an object only the way it was originally intended.Æ

So, let's not get overly fixated on using just words and numbers in text labels, and let's not limit our thinking to a single KPI indicator like ▲ or ▼.  Let's also not fixate on circles and dots as mark types; triangles work just as well! Should they be used as your dashboard standard? Probably not - they don't render well together and they're unusual, butÆ the point is that other shapes may be applicable in some circumstances.Æ

Can we have any shapes we can think of?Æ Æ
Well... no. There is more than one hitch to all this. You probably want to limit your shapes to existing keyboard characters  (more on that later) or to Unicode symbols, which are not the same as Unicode emojis. Not all emojis render on  desktop, which can make development challenging, requiring you to publish at every step to see if your content is displaying correctly. If you press Windows-. (period) in a Windows environment, the Emoji picker is displayed. Any item from the "Symbols" section should render in Mark Labels on both desktop and server. You'll  have to engage in trial and error to get the symbols and positioning that you like.Æ

Is there related material?Æ Æ
Check out this Wikipedia page on functional fixedness:Æ
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Functional_fixednessÆ

You can view large swaths of Unicode symbols at once at the following link:Æ
https://jrgraphix.net/r/Unicode/25A0-25FFÆ

Donut and pies! Oh, no! Anything but that!

What's going on with this specific example?Æ Æ
Obviously, the first thing going on is poking the bear with anyone who is a no-pie/no-donut absolutist. 😊 You can always click elsewhere if you don't like it....Æ Æ
Æ Æ Æ
There are a couple ideas to offer here. First, the center of donuts is often used for a KPI number that simply sits on the dashboard-wide background color, but maybe we can get more out of that center. Here we have a Real Mark as a BIG dot that acts as a heatmap of that often-underutilized territory. Within that we have indicators for whether the Region/Category intersection is not profitable and what quartile the order volume falls into vs. the rest of the donuts. The quartile concept is a bit of a contrived example, but it shows an alternative use of that real estate that does not significantly obscure the heat map shading. Most importantly, it shows that a Faux Mark can take on different shapes based on a single calculated field.Æ
Æ Æ Æ
Why the radio button instead of a full circle?Æ Æ
Remember how I said that symbols may need some adjustment? Well, there is no full circle symbol that is aligned with the same spacing as the quartile symbols – that’s a big design flaw by whoever established the unicode symbol standard. So, the radio buttons at least render in the same position and look somewhat like a filled circle. It’s a design compromise that's a bit disappointing.Æ Æ
Æ Æ Æ
Wait a second, a donut is already a dual-axis, and you're labeling part of the donut, so where is that "Not Profitable" circle coming from?Æ Æ
Ah, I’m glad that you noticed. The red circle is a Faux Mark on the underlying pie/donut. The mark label for the majority portion of the donut is hijacked and moved to the center of the circle to display the big red  circle where applicable. The minority portion of the donut still displays a KPI value in the upper left for the part we care about. Æ

Varying Color and Size on Faux Marks Requires Some Work.

What's going on with this specific example?Æ Æ Æ Æ
Admittedly, this chart is trying to do too much, but what is it trying to do? Mostly it's trying to show that Faux Marks can range over different sizes and different colors, but in order to do so, you need a calculated field per color or size  (there is one exception that we will cover later). You need to pile all those fields in the label, side-by-side, on the same line, and either size them or color them with different fonts. So, unless you want to create hundreds of calculated fields to generate the appearance of continuous colors and sizes, you are better off setting your sights on a handful of discrete options. For example, you may want to vary size by ordinal data like first, second, and third  or  S, M, L, XL. It's critical that those calculations are mutually exclusive, returning null when the size- or color-specific mark is not applicable.Æ
Æ Æ
Those Faux circles don't look the same as the others. What happened?Æ
I didn't like the way that the Unicode solid circles and pluses rendered - they were too off-center. Because I had already learned that basic text centers better, I tried a normal capital O and + from Tableau Book, but again it was not so great. After multiple trials, some with monospaced fonts like Consolas and Courier New, I settled on the thickish bold text of Arial, which centers rather nicely on Server.Æ

Is there anything else to note?Æ Æ
Profit Ratio is a bit wonky here because it can go negative. So, big blue means big positive and big red means big negative.Æ

Also, on a production dashboard, we would probably want to surface the cutoff values for the various discount ranges, but they are not really required here to demonstrate the intended point.Æ

Conditional Border Highlighting - A Rare Mark Option

What's going on with this specific example?Æ Æ Æ Æ
In this example, we have conditional borders that highlight... well... a highlight table. The naming is more confusing than the concept. Anyhow, most people dismiss this idea as impossible because conditional borders are not native functionality and as a result, it's not often seen. However, it's an effective example of divergent thinking. Æ

The border highlight is the second bar of a dual-axis with a blank color but a red border. If the "deep discount" (>25%) applies, the border is established at a length of 0.97 vs. 1.0 for the main bars of the highlight table, which is necessary in this specific sizing to get the full wraparound and not have the border cut off. If deep discount does not apply, the calculation returns null and thus there is no mark to display.Æ

I only see three marks: two Real, one Faux. Are you short-changing us?Æ
We are paying a price for that conditional border. Because the border can't be colored conditionally in a native manner, we are removing the entire mark when it doesn't apply. With no Real Mark, we have nothing for the Faux Mark to piggyback upon. So, if there were a Faux Mark symbol that would apply to a subset of the border condition, it could be added. Here I add the actual deep discount amount as simple text.Æ
Æ Æ
Couldn't you use better symbols for the most profitable ship modes?Æ
I tried. This graph is where I rediscovered that emojis often don't render on desktop mark labels, so my idea of using a series of icons (airplane, truck, turtle, snail) did not work out. I also did not want to "go to the well a second time" with the quarter-filled circles, so I settled for what I have here and gave them additional text abbreviations of the ship mode on the labels. At least it serves as a second example of shape on a Faux Mark.Æ

Example 7: Bar Hashing (Bar, Dot, Gantt Bar, Hash)

Quadruple-Axis
Inspiration: Lindsay Betzendahl's vizzendata.com blog

Æ Æ
An old trick becomes a new trick!

What's going on with this specific example?Æ Æ Æ Æ
Have you ever seen an old textbook or report that pre-dates the widespread use of color printing? How did they differentiate dimensions on bars? The used hashing, right? (Or "hatching" elsewhere in the English-speaking world.) We can still do that, and we can accomplish it with one of the available Faux Marks in a dual-axis. The bonus is that hashing adds information without burning our retinas with additional colors!Æ

Okay, so how did you do it?Æ
The trick here is to make a conditional text bar of hashing characters that is large enough to cover the bar and then color the text in the label to match the background. As you can see in the example, it covers negative values well. Many symbols were tried, and the best candidates are listed in the parameter.Æ
Æ Æ
Are there any caveats?Æ
There are a few. If the bars are too thick, the hashing on the first and last bars seems to drift, being stuck between either not being large enough to cover the bar, or when actually large enough, overlapping neighboring bars - at least for this data. So, keep the bars on the thinner side. Also, X symbols and letters did not work well. You could try other symbols or standard keyboard characters, but the selected symbols seem to require fewer characters than the following standard keyboard options:  / \ | { }.Æ

So why are there two graphs - a triple-axis and a quadruple-axis?Æ
In this scenario, a triple is way easier than attempting a second Faux Mark on the left. Here's the situation: we are being constrained by that cool conditional coloring of the mark label to match the Real Gantt Mark indicator at the left end of the bar. Any additional Faux Mark would be of the same color, which could cause confusion if added. So, as-is, it's probably not a good idea. In the quadruple, we circumvent that by a) switching the Mark Label Font from Match Mark Color to Automatic b) creating three calculated fields: one for a below goal red label, one for above goal green, and one for the additional indicator c) creating a calc to get the spacing right d) stacking all calcs on one line in the label and coloring the individual fields in the text e) setting the font to a monospaced one (Consolas). Even then, similar appearing symbols varied the spacing. I could have used the same symbol twice with different colors, but I decided to go with a simple *.Æ

Haven't I seen that Gannt hack before?Æ
You may well have!  Included below is a link to Lindsay Betzendahl's blog for Gantt indicators. Here the Gantt is flipped to the left of the bar, and a text label is added because that thin bar doesn't show much color. We could use a standard Bar with a value of 0, but that doubles the width, and makes it look like a thin stacked bar segment.Æ
https://vizzendata.com/2024/05/29/coloring-the-ends-of-a-bar-chart-in-tableau/Æ

You could also use a Gantt Bar as a sort of Axis Ruler, like this example by guest blogger Pradeep G Kumar:Æ
https://www.flerlagetwins.com/2020/04/si-eff-simple-effective-design-approach.html

One Measure Growing Out of Another

What's going on with this specific example?Æ Æ Æ Æ
L-Bars are captivating. Thanks to Sarah Pallett and her predecessors for promoting them. In this example, the L-Bar of sales springs forth from a dot/shape of order volume. Those are our Real Marks. A Faux Mark of +/- to the left of the order volume dot indicates whether the Sub-Category is profitable. Lastly, a checkmark is our final Faux Mark, indicating at the far right whether the sales goal was met. The main idea here is to keep your eyes open for ways to extend new graph types with additional marks.Æ
Æ Æ Æ
How does that L-Bar work?Æ Æ
The Real Mark type is Line, and the vertical portion comes from selecting the Path Line Type of Step. The height is a function of the row height and Measure Names being on rows to produce two rows. The helper measure, MAX(0.0), in our Measure Values card gives our line an origin from which to spring. Having the MAX(0.0) as a dual-axis gives us a point upon which to hang our order volume. And, as in our other examples, our Faux Marks piggyback on the Real Marks.Æ Æ

Some Sub-Categories are missing! Where did they go?Æ Æ
Some Sub-Categories were removed for aesthetic purposes so that a scroll bar wouldn't appear.Æ Æ

Is there any other formatting trickery going on here?Æ Æ
Yes, the Labels on the dots have an alignment of Middle Left, and the text itself has initial spacing from left, then the Faux Mark, then spacing to bridge the dot area, followed by the Sub-Category name and actual order count. It's like the approach from Bar Hashing, but without the complicated spacer calculation.Æ Æ
Æ Æ Æ
Where can I find the work that inspired this?Æ Æ

Are Three Sets of Two Trends Too Much?

What's going on with this specific example?Æ Æ Æ Æ
Here we have a dual-axis bar-in-bar vs. line, colored by East vs. West Regions. On top of that, we have dot trends for Profit and a Faux Mark for whether a sales goal was met. The kicker here is that the dots are actually reference lines, so, we actually have one Faux Mark still in reserve! With reference lines, you can add as many trends as you want, but it's probably already too busy. Note that the line trend is parameterized and shares counts that all have the same units, and the axis range resizes automatically.Æ Æ
Æ Æ Æ
So, what's the catch?Æ Æ
There are a couple catches. The Date pill must be discrete for the Reference Line of the dots to compute correctly when set to Per Cell. Also, it’s difficult to hover over those reference lines, so their values are repeated in the tooltips of both the bars and the lines.Æ Æ

What if I like the Measure Values method of bar-in-bar?Æ Æ Æ Æ
I have recreated the chart using Measure Values for the bar-in-bar and retained the parameterized measure as the dual-axis. The second axis is dynamic, but that axis can only be a single measure. We only have one Measure Values shelf, so having East and West on the second axis as individual Measure Values can't happen. However, to further the point about unlimited marks being possible with reference lines, the horizontal lines here duplicate the line series of the dynamic measure.Æ

Æ Æ
Join our fun foray into formatting with four or more mark types!

First, what is a dual-axis chart?Æ Æ
Dual-axis charts in Tableau are charts that have two fused measures on either the Rows or Columns shelf. They can be used in a variety of ways, such as:Æ Æ
● Comparing two measures with different scales like sales and order volume over time. Æ
● Displaying different types of marks, such as bars and lines, for the same or different measures. Æ
● Simply improving the look and feel of a dashboard. Æ
Æ Æ
Our examples in this workbook are concentrated on the second and third bullet points above.Æ Æ

What about synchronizing axes?Æ Æ
If both measures share the same units and are approximately the same order of magnitude, the axes can and should be synchronized (meaning set to the same range) to avoid confusion. Obviously, if we are duplicating a single measure, the synchronization is usually a given.Æ Æ

But the examples are not really a quadruple-axis, right?Æ Æ
In a strict sense, no, they're not. Most examples are dual-axis, the marks of which are called "Real Marks" within this workbook. Two additional "Faux Marks" created from text symbols piggyback on the labels of the Real Marks. The title of each dashboard explains the implementation by using the following format: Example [N]: [Name] ([Real Mark 1], [Faux Mark 1], [Real Mark 2], [Faux Mark 2]). Every example is a single chart though; none is a dual-axis layered over another dual-axis. Each example also has legends to explain the chart and one observational annotation.Æ

Isn’t this bad design? Aren’t you creating cognitive overload with piling all this stuff on one chart? Isn't this just "chart junk"?Æ
Maybe so, but the goal here is to show what's possible and to inspire developers to use Tableau in ways they had not previously imagined. So, the charts presented here definitely do not represent ideal design practices. Take what works and what is useful. Ignore the rest. Chart junk? One man's junk is another man's treasure.Æ

Why’d you use Superstore?Æ Æ
The ideas presented require data with a certain minimum complexity, and Superstore contains sufficient dimensions and measures to generate the intended number of mark types, so we examine Superstore orders in ten different ways.Æ

What's up with the crayon and comic sans font?Æ
I'm trying to promote an air of creative fun that many of us may have not tapped into fully since childhood.
Summary, Warnings, and Alternatives

Hopefully, dear reader, by this point you have experienced a few "Wait. What?" moments and are ready to try some of these approaches on your own projects.Æ
Æ
Remember:Æ
● Quadruple-axis charts are really dual-axis charts with characters on the labels of the Real Marks that act as supplemental Faux Marks.Æ
● The rendering order of mark layers from bottom to top is: left pill mark, right pill mark, left pill text, right pill text.Æ
● It's best to limit the functionality of the third and higher mark types to show exceptions. This helps to avoid visually overwhelming the user.Æ
● You may want to create one show/hide parameter per Faux Mark so that they can be disabled like map layers for simplified viewing.Æ
● Faux Marks are good for data that is a 1:1 attribute of the Level of Detail of the viz. Examples: this product is (profitable or not), this region's sales goal was (met or not met).Æ
● You can always combine regular text labels with Faux Marks.Æ
Æ
Warnings:Æ
● Formatting requires some tinkering and may not be pixel-perfect. You may have to get really creative with alignment when combining Faux Marks with text.Æ
● You won't be able to disallow the overlapping of other labels because if you do, you'll lose your Faux Mark that resides in that label entirely.Æ
● Faux Marks have the same tooltip as the rest of the text that shares the Label.Æ
● There are no borders on Faux Marks, so if you like crisp outlines on dots, you need to you make one with one Faux Mark layered over another.Æ
Æ
Alternatives:Æ
● You could push all Faux Marks to tooltips, thus decluttering the views, but out of sight may be out of mind.Æ
● The view could be divided into small multiples with rows and columns for the various indicator values.Æ
● You could redo all these charts with map layers if you're willing to do the latitude and longitude calculations and reverse the x and y coordinates in your mind. Æ
● True quadruple-axis charts could be implemented by layering two dual-axis worksheets. The pies at the top of this workbook are an example: https://public.tableau.com/app/profile/ray3296/viz/AnimalEdibility-MoMWeek72024/WouldYouEatThatÆ Æ
● You can use a relation to an "expander" table (data densification) to create multiple marks, like these crazy donuts that have a ring for every 100%:Æ
https://public.tableau.com/app/profile/ray3296/viz/Piesover100MoMUKStudyAbroad/StudyAbroadÆ
● In your data prep, you could create additional rows for more marks at the same plot points or create more measures and plot them concentrically using Measure Values.Æ
Æ
That's all folks. Build to learn!Æ

The single data source (Orders / Sample Superstore) is a Tableau EXTRACT (.hyper) with no live upstream. Built as a Power BI IMPORT table from the already-materialized extract CSV (data/ds.orders_sample_superstore.csv, 9994 rows) via the DataFolder M parameter. Repoint the partition if a real upstream exists.

NO marked Date table was added, by design. The workbook has no time-intelligence / prior-period / YoY logic; every date use is a simple axis bucket (Ship Date->month, Order Date->quarter). A marked Date table would (a) impose cross-filtering the source never had and (b) risk the DATESBETWEEN axis-wiping trap on window measures. Date bucketing is done with plain calc columns on Orders (Ship Month, Order Quarter) - which is exactly what the window table-calcs address over.

1 fact table (Orders) + 4 disconnected parameter tables; ZERO relationships. Faithful to Tableau (single source, 0 joins/blends). Parameter tables are intentionally disconnected slicers read via SELECTEDVALUE value-measures.

CORE OF THIS WORKBOOK + KEY REPORT-LAYER HANDOFF. It is a catalog of 'quadruple/multi-axis' tricks: Tableau fakes 3rd..6th axes by stacking measures on Rows/Columns (dual-axis) PLUS constant 'anchor' placeholders (MAX(1.0)/MAX(0.0)/0) on extra axes, then layering unicode SHAPE MARKS driven by table calcs. Power BI combo charts support AT MOST a DUAL axis. The DATA behind every mark is modeled here as measures; the literal N-axis stacking is a REPORT-LAYER concern and a hard capability bound. Report builder guidance: recreate each figure as a combo chart (line + column, 2 axes max) or small-multiple / overlaid transparent visuals; the constant anchors are provided as [Axis Anchor One]=1 and [Axis Anchor Zero]=0 for secondary-axis scaling if needed.

PARSER GAP (known): dual-axis / multi-pane worksheets are flattened to the first pane/mark card. E.g. rows=[SUM(Sales),SUM(Sales)] in Area+Dot+Line+Circle and Circle+Dot+Line+Circle is a collapsed DUAL-axis; columns=[SUM(Sales),SUM(Sales)] in the Scatter sheets is a dual x-axis. The real per-axis measures and mark cards were recovered by reading the .twb XML directly. Full per-pane mark encodings (which glyph on which axis) remain a report-layer reconstruction task.

PARSER GAP: every calculated field's `formula` is null in migration-spec.json. All 71 formulas (plus 11 helper/anchor calcs) were recovered from the .twbx -> .twb XML <calculation formula='...'> and saved to data/_calc_formulas.json, which was the actual source of truth for translation.

The 16 table calcs' addressing (partition/axis) was RECOVERED from the surviving worksheet `encodings` blocks (not guessed): Map Rows/Columns INDEX() over State (States ws); Dot/Circle Sales windows over Ship Month (Area/Circle+Dot+Line, Region=South); Sales East/West + 5th/6th-axis windows over Order Quarter (Bar-in-Bar ws, Region in {East,West}); Bar/Triangles/Dot/Hash Profit(-Ratio) windows over Sub-Category (SubCat Bar / L-Bar). One residual inference: Tableau's LAST()/sort DIRECTION for non-date axes (Sub-Category) is assumed alphabetical (the default table addressing); re-verify if a report sorts a pane by measure.

FAITHFUL-QUIRK PRESERVED + FLAG TO CUSTOMER. In Bar Profit Window, Triangles/Dot/Hash Profit Ratio Window the 'Control' band's UPPER bound uses WINDOW_STDEV(SUM([Sales])) while the measured value is Profit / Profit Ratio (the lower bound correctly uses the measure's own stdev). This is a copy-paste bug in the source workbook. Translated faithfully (VAR SPlus over [Total Sales], VAR SMinus over the measure) rather than silently 'fixed'; recommend the customer confirm intent.

NUMERIC GROUND-TRUTH (data/ ..., _validation/groundtruth_tablecalcs.py) re-computed key table calcs two independent ways (Tableau semantics vs a literal DAX-mechanics replica) and matched to <1e-6: (1) Map Tile Grid over 49 states alphabetical - Alabama=(col0,row0), Georgia=(col9,row0), Idaho=(col0,row1), Wyoming=(col8,row4); (2) Sales East/West Max = $98,023.26 (peak quarter 2024-Q4), identical with/without the Region in {East,West} filter; (3) Late % = 29.06% (2,904 late of 9,994); (4 bonus) Order Volume rank over Sub-Category - Binders=1 (n=1523), Paper=2, Furnishings=3.

[On Time Ship %] = AVG(IF <on-time SLA> THEN 1 END) has NO ELSE branch, so late rows are null and Tableau AVG ignores them -> the value trends to 1 on any all-on-time context. Translated faithfully as AVERAGEX('Orders', IF(<cond>,1)) (DAX AVERAGEX also ignores blanks). Flag as a likely source bug (intended ELSE 0 gives a true on-time rate); the row-level [On Time Ship?] column supports a correct rate if desired.

5 worksheets use the Tableau Measure Names/Measure Values virtual pivot (Bar-in-Bar Measure Values: Sales West/East; L-Bar: Sales + a calc; Ship Mode Test Sheet: Profit + 2 LOD sums; SubCat Bar; On Time Ship Pies). No native Power BI equivalent - the [:Measure Names] pseudo-field is excluded from the model. Report phase: bind each resolved measure directly (one field per axis series) or use a Field Parameter.

Several worksheets fabricate axes/marks with REFERENCE LINES labeled by unicode glyphs (Bar-in-Bar: max/average lines labeled with '-' and dot marks; SubCat Bar: 'Goal: <Value>' average line from [Parameter 3]). These are report-layer analytics (Power BI reference lines / error bars / constant lines) - modeled here only via their backing values (e.g. [Profit Ratio Goal Value], [Sales East/West Max], [bar-in-bar negative offset]).

~30 translated measures return UNICODE SHAPE/SYMBOL STRINGS (dots the marks: circle, filled-circle, triangle, bar, star, quartile arcs, hatching, indicator grids, +/- symbols, check/asterisk). These are visual encodings meant for Tableau's shape/text marks. In Power BI they are best realized as conditional formatting / custom icons / measure-driven markers at the report layer; they are provided as measures so the report can bind them, but the exact mark placement is a report-layer task.

[Not Profitable Circle - Exclude Ship] and [Profitable - Exclude Ship] use LOD {EXCLUDE [On Time Ship?]: ...}, a pie label-centering trick (compute ignoring the on-time split). Translated with CALCULATE(..., REMOVEFILTERS('Orders'[On Time Ship?])). Cosmetic; verify if surfaced on a real visual.

4 Tableau parameters -> 4 disconnected single-column slicer tables with SELECTEDVALUE value-measures: 'Select Highlight Function' ([Parameter 2], list Max/Min/Max & Min/Last/Control/All/None, default Max) drives all window highlight marks; 'Profit Ratio Goal' ([Parameter 3], REAL RANGE default 0.12 - approximated as a discrete list 0..0.30); 'Select Hashing' ([Parameter 4], list of hatch patterns, default '/// '); 'Variable Metric' ([Parameter 5], list None/-Order/-Customer/-Product Count, default '- Order Count'). (No 'Parameter 1' exists in the source.)

Two Tableau fields had BRACKET-LADEN auto-generated captions illegal as measure names and were renamed: 'WINDOW_MAX(MAX([Sales - East],[Sales - West]))' -> [Sales East/West Window Max] (a thin alias of [Sales East/West Max], its identical twin); '[5th/6th Axis Metric - East]/WINDOW_MAX([5th/6th Axis Metric - East])' -> [Axis Metric East Normalized]. Also '5th/6th Axis Metric - MAX East/West' -> [Axis Metric MAX East/West]. All DAX references use the new names.

Heavy Ctrl-drag duplication left internal names systematically mismatched to captions (e.g. internal '[5th Axis Metric - East (copy)_3743054306899050499]' has caption 'Variable Metric - West'; 'Sales - 2017 (copy)...' = 'Sales - East'). Every translation resolved fields via the parser CAPTION, never the internal name, so operand identity is correct despite the scramble.

5 worksheet-referenced calcs are NOT in the spec fields[] (parser dropped them): they are the constant fake-axis anchors MAX(1.0) (Highlight Table), MAX(0.0) (L-Bar), 0.0 x2 (On Time Ship Pies), 0 (SubCat Bar). Modeled as [Axis Anchor One]=1 and [Axis Anchor Zero]=0 (collapsed) and documented as report-layer secondary-axis scaffolding (see multiaxis.capability_boundary).

Postal Code kept as TEXT to preserve leading-zero ZIPs (no calc uses it numerically). F22 (=Ship Date + ~2556 days, an Excel export artifact referenced by nothing) retained HIDDEN for lineage. Row ID hidden. Import types set with an explicit 'en-US' culture in every Table.TransformColumnTypes (guards against custom-locale refresh failures).

All 71 spec calculated fields dispositioned (coverage asserted in generate_tmdl.py): 2 became CALC COLUMNS (row-level: Days to Ship, On Time Ship?); 69 became MEASURES (aggregations / references to measures / table calcs / parameters). Row-level date buckets Ship Month & Order Quarter added as helper calc columns for window addressing. Aggregating fields -> measures; pure-cosmetic string/shape fields also modeled as measures but flagged report-layer (see report.shape_mark_string_measures).

No live Power BI Desktop / MCP / EVALUATE was available. Validation = (1) STRUCTURAL: TmdlSerializer.DeserializeDatabaseFromFolder -> OK (5 tables, 30 columns incl. 4 calculated, 79 measures, 0 relationships, compat 1606) PLUS integrity asserts: model-wide measure-name uniqueness, no measure==column name per table, every DAX [bracket] token resolves, AND description coverage 100% (tables 5/5, columns 30/30, measures 79/79) via the tmdl_validate --require-descriptions gate; (2) NUMERIC: Python ground-truth of 4 table calcs two independent ways, all PASS (see tablecalc.groundtruth). Measures were NOT executed in a live engine; a Desktop refresh + spot-check is recommended before publishing.

AI/COPILOT READINESS PASS (post-build). Every table (5), column (30 incl. 4 calc) and measure (79) carries a business-meaning `description` - TMDL /// maps to TOM .Description, which DAX Copilot reads (first ~200 chars). Every categorical/dimension column lists its enum domain: Ship Mode (First Class/Same Day/Second Class/Standard Class), Segment (Consumer/Corporate/Home Office), Region (Central/East/South/West), Category (Furniture/Office Supplies/Technology), Sub-Category (17 values), Country (United States); high-cardinality columns (State 49, City 531, Customer 793, Product 1862, IDs) describe cardinality + role instead of enumerating. The 4 disconnected-parameter slicer columns list their option domains. Business-queryable measures lead with plain meaning, faithful Tableau-formula provenance retained after; visual-encoding/glyph helper measures keep their purpose descriptions. Natural-language aliases folded into prose (Sales a.k.a. revenue, Profit a.k.a. margin, State a.k.a. province). Authored in generate_tmdl.py (single source of truth) and persisted to fabric/QuadrupleAxisCharts.SemanticModel/definition/ - NOT via an MCP ExportToTmdlFolder round-trip (no live MCP/Desktop here; the round-trip would cosmetically reformat and desync the generator). Coverage enforced by tmdl_validate --require-descriptions (100%).

Deliberately NOT set in this pass (no stable offline Git/file contract, or Fabric-service-side): (1) AI data schema / linguistic modeling (LSDL synonyms schema) - no stable committable TMDL contract; NL aliases folded into description prose instead. (2) Semantic-model 'AI instructions' text - no committable file contract. (3) Verified answers - not Git-supported. (4) 'Approved for Copilot' / semantic-model indexing / Copilot enablement - Fabric service settings applied AFTER publish, not in model files. Apply these in the Power BI / Fabric service post-deployment.

Ex.1 Line/Area++ & Ex.2 Line+++ stack an Area + Dot + Line + Circle (and a deep-discount Dot) as 4-5 synthetic mark layers pinned on ONE sales axis via duplicated Rows measures. Power BI caps at dual-axis; rebuilt as a single lineChart over Ship Month with markers, binding the real windowed measures (Total Sales, Dot Sales Window, Circle Sales Window, Deep Discount Dot). The layered area-fill + N stacked mark geometry is NOT reproduced (capability boundary).

Ex.7 Bar Hashing fakes a quad-axis by stacking Profit Ratio windows (Triangles/Dot/Hash Profit Ratio Window measures) plus hatch/hash fills driven by the Select Hashing param. Rebuilt as two dual-axis combos: Profit Ratio columns vs a dynamic Profit Ratio Goal Value line on a shared axis. Hatch/hash fills and the N>2 synthetic axis stack are NOT reproducible in native Power BI (capability boundary).

Ex.9 Bar/Line/Dot (Bar-in-Bar Measure vs Dynamic + Measure Values) overlays different-WIDTH bars on a shared axis (a wide Sales bar behind a narrow Variable Metric bar) plus unicode reference marks - a geometry Power BI cannot encode. Rebuilt as a dual-axis combo (Total Sales columns + Variable Metric COUNTD line) over Order Quarter, plus a clustered column of Sales West/East (the resolved Measure Names/Values fields). Overlapping-width bar-in-bar geometry is a capability boundary.

The constant axis-anchor measures that make the fake axes line up (Axis Anchor One = MAX(1.0), Axis Anchor Zero = MAX(0.0)/0, and the normalized 5th/6th-axis pins) exist in the model but are intentionally NOT bound to any visual: Power BI has no synthetic secondary/tertiary axis to pin a constant to, so an anchor placeholder has no native target. They are the mechanism of the N-axis trick and are dropped in the report layer (capability boundary).

Ex.3 Triangles x4 uses Tableau 'Shape' marks (four triangle glyphs per Sub-Category across profit-ratio windows). Rebuilt as a Sales-vs-Profit scatter by Sub-Category (South), bubble size = Quantity, preserving the underlying data. Arbitrary unicode/shape mark geometry and the 4 stacked windows are NOT reproducible as native data marks (capability boundary).

Ex.8 L-Bar/Dot draws an L-shaped bar with check/dot glyph marks; several sheets also encode unicode indicator strings (Ship Mode Most Profitable Icon, Profit Ratio +/- Symbol, Star Rating, 3x3/1x9 indicator grids, Select Hashing glyphs). Rebuilt Ex.8 as a dual-axis combo (Total Sales columns + Order Count line). String/glyph indicator measures cannot drive PBIR mark geometry or data-color rules and render as plain text/labels only (capability boundary).

Ex.10 Map Trellis renders 49 state Multipolygon mini-maps arranged in an INDEX-derived tile grid (Map Rows/Map Columns). Rebuilt as a scatterChart tile-grid (X=Map Columns, Y=Map Rows inverted, Category=State, size=Sales) preserving the exact 49-cell layout. The per-cell real geography (a small choropleth per state) is dropped; a single azureMap choropleth over State is the alternative but is not a trellis-of-maps (capability boundary).

Ex.4 Varying Shape draws a pie per Region x Category (On Time Ship Pies). pieChart has no Small-Multiples role, so rebuilt as a hundredPercentStackedColumnChart (X=Region, legend=On Time Ship?, Y=Order Count) small-multipled by Category - faithful on-time-vs-late proportion. The pie-mark (angular) geometry is approximated by stacked columns.

Ex.6 Border Highlight uses colored cell BORDERS (a 3rd visual encoding) plus most-profitable ship-mode icons on a Sub-Category x Region highlight table. Rebuilt as a pivotTable with a linearGradient3 background FillRule on Total Profit. Border-as-encoding and the per-cell icon set are not reproduced (Power BI cell formatting has no border-value channel).

Several worksheets carry UNRESOLVED calc references inside their Measure Names/Measure Values pivots that the parser could not resolve to a model field: Highlight Table (a column calc), L-Bar (a column calc), On Time Ship Pies (angle/color calcs), SubCat Bar w/Hash Indicator & Hash Quad (a Quantity/Sales hash calc), Ship Mode Test Sheet (a label calc). Resolved pivot fields were bound directly; unresolved ones were NOT guessed, so those visuals may be missing a secondary field. Surfaced for review rather than fabricated.

Ex.5 Color & Size: Tableau's continuous color-by-Discount + size ramp is rebuilt as a scatter with bubble size = Deep Discount (Highlight) and a linearGradient3 dataPoint FillRule on the same measure (projected so it can drive color). Faithful double-encoding; the exact Tableau color ramp is approximated by a green->amber->orange saturation gradient.

The 4 disconnected parameter tables (Select Highlight Function, Profit Ratio Goal, Select Hashing, Variable Metric; 0 relationships by design) are rendered as single-select dropdown slicers with default selections matching the Tableau current values (Max / 0.12 / hatch glyph / - Order Count). Being disconnected they only feed their SELECTEDVALUE measures and cannot cross-filter the Orders fact - expected for a parameter, noted for traceability.
